title: Oracle Of Music Some Thoughts On The Hor
author: Unknown

body.text:
Oracle of Music 1
Some thoughts on the horoscope of Claudio Monteverdi
Angela Voss On 21st May 2014 Ihappened to turn on Radio 3, and was greeted by amagnificent performance of
the early baroque composer Claudio Mo te edis Il Combattimento di Tancredi e Clorinda, amoving
setti gof Tasso spoe aout a Ch istia kight iad ete tl sla igthe Musli oahe lo es.2 Spellbound, Ilistened to the restof the programme, which featured Monte edi as opose of the
eek, and was particularly moved by the intensity and power of his music. Later Iconsulted the
ephemeris and was astounded to see that during that very week Mercury was transiting over
Mo te edis Ve us/Me uoju tio, hi halso happe sto straddle my descendant at 22 Gemini. By what agency were Mercury, Monteverdi, myself and the BBC being brought into 1 Thus named by fellow musician Benedetto Ferrari in 1640.
2 Torquato Tasso, Gerusalemme Liberata, 1581, set by Monteverdi in his Eighth Book of Madrigals, 1638. 1 alignment? It was one of those spine-tingling moments when astrological symbolism becomes
ealised as oete, and two orders of reality meet across an impossible divide. In touching on afew key themes of Mo te edisho os ope, Iatto de ost ate ho this
revelatory potential of astrological symbolism may help us understand the inner landscape of his
creative genius, as he strove to enhance the affective power of song through the perfect marriage of
words and music.
Claudio Monteverdi was born at 9.30 am on 15th May 1567, in the north Italian town of Cremona.3
He is regarded by musicologists as the eato of ode usi;4 straddling Renaissance and baroque eras, he pioneered anew form of dramatic singing which led to the developmentof opera
ada a f othe old st le of pol pho iopositio. Th oughout Mo te edis aee efi d a

radical juxtaposition of the Renaissance world-view of magical sympathy and correspondence (most
evident in the polyphonic madrigals and motets) with the suspe sio of dis elief de aded the new form of dramatic monody, the stile rappresentativo, that informs his later works.5 Yet he
manages to hold aprecarious tension between the old and the new, atension that was to give way,
in the end, to an infinite nostalgia for the lost magical power of music in an Enlightened world.6
So what does his astrology show us? Ihave already pointed to the most striking factor: Mo te edis
Mercury/Venus conjunction in Gemini, areflection of his extraordinary gift for sensual arousal
through music. We can see Mercury as the genius of the composer, an alchemical agentof
transformation, translating into sound atranscendent beauty – for Venus brings Neptune in her
wake. Monteverdi was adamant that in his eusi the words should command the harmony, which is nicely demonstrated by the strength of Mercury in his own sign. In this he understood
himself to be afollower of Plato, fo ho the aeof the di tio adthe ods follo a dconform to the disposition of the soul. 7
In Greek mythology, Venus and Mercury unite and produce Eros, the daimon of Love; in another tale
their child is Hermaphroditus. These two archetypal principles can be characterised in many ways –
for example, form and spirit, love and intellect, imagination and reason or image and interpretation. 3

The birth time is calculated according to the Julian calendar and was sourced from
http://www.astrotheme.com/astrology/Claudio_Monteverdi (accessed 13/12/14). Icannot find further confirmation of the
time, however it produces an astrological profile that clearly fits with Mo te edispe so alit adith the ufoldi gof
his life events. In my view, astrological symbolism lives and becomes meaningful through engagement and intention,
therefore Iam taking this birth chart as alikel sto adsuspe di gthe eui eent for proof.
4 See L. Shrade, 1950, Monteverdi, Creator of Modern Music, New York: Norton & Co.
5 For further exploration of this theme, See G. Tomlinson, 1993, Music in Renaissance Magic, University of Chicago Press,
ch.7.
6 “ee D. Chua,, Vi ezo Galilei, Mode it adthe Di isio of Natu e i “. Cla k & A. ‘ehdi g, eds, Music Theory
and Natural order from the Renaissance to the early twentieth century, Cambridge University Press, ch.1.
7 G. Tomlinson, 1990, Monteverdi and the End of the Renaissance, Oxford University Press, 25. 2 Thei oju tio appea sto ge eate athi d piiple hi hifuses beauty with meaning,
creating asymbol which can carry the highest potentialities for psychic integration.8 Anyone who has
e eee d ato Mo te edis usi ill su el testif to the etao di aeoti ha ge

embodied in the songs of the yearning lover. Through the opose satful use of musical
suspension and resolution, this charge is at times visceral in its intensity, evoking asynthesis of
emotional and spiritual passion which is atonce painful and cathartic.9 We can see this quality in the
Venus/Mercury square to Pluto in the eighth house, adding hidden depths to a Geminian ingenuity
which, arguably, is not so successful when light and playful. This aspect also signifies areligious
fervour and emotional intensity through innovation and versatility, demonstrated to perfection in
the Vespers of 1610.
Furthermore, this conjunction is opposed by Uranus in the fifth house – Mo te edis eial of the
aie t Geek ideal of ethos as ahie ed th ough innovative vocal techniques, instrumentation and performance practice. His new music was named the seconda prattica ose o d
pati eto disti guish it f ousi al the olde prima prattica of the Renaissance polyphonists, and he spoke out (Mercury/Uranus) against reactionary critics. The most famous of these was the theorist
Giovanni Atusi, ho iith tasiti g “atu atta kothe ipe fe tio sof ode usi opposi g Mo te edis Ma slau hed aiious hi hihis ie iolated taditio al ules ith

unwarranted passion and affectation.10 Artusi became the voice of Saturn in Libra, extolling harmony
a dpopo tio ithe fa eof Mo te edispio ee i g Aies Ma s, hi halso pla ed out ihis oted
irascibility and impatience. Mars influences Mercury/Venus by sextile, and indeed the new music
was intimately concerned with the individual; the passion and suffering of Orpheus and Arianna for
example, each of whom lamented their lost love but were redeemed by agod. Perhaps Monteverdi
was dramatizing his own suffering, of which there was plenty in his life, both financial and
emotional, signified by his Moon/Saturn in second house Virgo. Iwill end my musings with some
thoughts about this painful conjunction.
1607 saw the production of the opera L’Orfeo. There is apoignant significance in the revival of the
story of the legendary musician whofailed to rescue his wife Eurydice from the underworld, at a
time when the divine feminine was becoming estranged from natural philosophy. It is as though
Monteverdi is struggling to hold on to the soul of the Renaissance, as Orpheus attempts to enchant 8 The Auiatio a dith of the di iehild aeead as pa allels i Ch istia
tholog. See also the relationship
et ee Ve us a d Me u i Botti ellispai ti g Primavera. Isubscribe to the theory put forward by John Dee that Venus is the Grace being
pie ed Eos a o a defla ed ith lo efo Me u. Both Botti elli and Monteverdi were inspired by neoplatonic ideals
of the transformative potential of the symbolic image, whether visual or auditory.
9 An example would be Hor che’lciel elaterra in the Eighth Book of Madrigals.
10 See Tomlinson, 1990, 21-29. 3

the world and reinstate the lost power of music. On the nightof the premiere, 24th February, we find
a Mars return for Monteverdi, emphasising the heroic questof Orpheus. His progressed ascendant is
on his progressed Saturn, his progressed Moon on his descendant. On this very day, his transiting
moon is also moving over his descendant, away from transiting Mercury and heading for the eighth
house. Mercury will not catch her, just as Mercury was not able to lead Eurydice outof the
underworld as she made the transition from being the wife of Orpheus into the house of Hades.
This as to esadl popheti, as Mo te edis oife Claudia died oth September 1607 leaving him with three young children. With his progressed ascendant approaching Saturn, he had
financial worries, illness and intense emotional strains to cope with. Beginning work on anew opera,
what theme could encapsulate the desolation of Moon/Saturn more appropriately than the story of
Ariadne, abandoned on the island of Naxos by Theseus, in despair about her fate? As the first
performance of the opera approached, Monteverdi was hit by another tragedy - his lead singer,
pupil and ward Caterina Martinelli, died of smallpox at the age of eighteen.11 Monteverdi reported
that the writing of Ariannanearly killed him, and in the famous lament (the only surviving piece of
the opera) he pulls out all the stops of expressive recitative. There was apparently not adry eye in
the audience, a d Aia a seotio al outpourings started anew operatic genre.12 She is the despai i g, la eti g, aado ed fe i i e, oth ahet pall a d i Mo te edispe so al life
circumstance, for his soul was abandoned by the two women who meant most to him.
How interesting it is that he re-wrote Aria a s La etas ala etof the Vi gi, fo outof this

immense personal sorrow redemption appears through his devotion to Mary. In 1610, as he was
writing his most fervent and inventive celebration of the divine feminine, the Vespro della Beata
Virgine, progressed Moon was conjuncting his natal Pluto and progressed Mercury conjunct natal
Mercury. In this, his most astonishing, avant-garde and innovative work, erotic and spiritual desire
fuse in music which dares to push all boundaries and challenge all conventions. Passionate, versatile,
innovative, dramatic and deeply religious, the Vespers can be seen as the highest expression of
Mo te edis Ve us-Mercury/Pluto/Uranus Tsquare, and aredemption of his worldly sorrow. 11 “ee E. “tai ha ps,, The Life a d Death of Cate i a Mati elli: Ne Lighto Mo te edis Arianna, Early Music
History, vol. 5, 155-186.
12 See T. Carter, 1999, La eti g Aiad e?, Early Music vol. 27, no.3, 395-405. 4
